key_points: RHS Criterion: Right angle, Hypotenuse, and one Side must be equal
AAS Criterion: A variation of ASA where two angles and a non-included side are equal
SSA and AAA are not valid criteria for congruence
Congruence is used to prove properties of isosceles and equilateral triangles
Complex figures can be decomposed into congruent triangles to find unknown values
examples: RHS: Two right triangles with hypotenuse $10\text{ cm}$ and one leg $6\text{ cm}$ are congruent
AAS: $\angle A=\angle D, \angle B=\angle E$ and $BC=EF$ (side not between angles)
Counter-example: Two triangles with angles $30^{\circ}, 60^{\circ}, 90^{\circ}$ but different sizes (AAA) are not congruent
Application: Proving that angles opposite to equal sides in a triangle are equal
formulas: Euler's Relation in Triangles $\rightarrow$ Sum of angles $= 180^{\circ}$ (used to convert AAS to ASA)
exam_tips: In RHS, always identify the hypotenuse (the side opposite the $90^{\circ}$ angle) first
Be careful not to use SSA; it is a common trap in multi-step geometry problems
When proving congruence in overlapping triangles, identify the common side or common angle
Use CPCT effectively to find lengths or angles in complex quadrilateral problems
